heading: SECONDO L'ARMONIA DEL DIAPASON
card_summary: This very long section (21 chapters, pp. 1095–1206) constitutes the cosmological and theological heart of the Primo Cantico, demonstrating that the universe is a musical instrument played by God and that all created things proceed from and return to the divine unity through the harmony of the diapason (octave). Zorzi draws on Orphic hymns, the Pythagorean Dorilao, Alexander of Miletus, Timaeus of Locri, Plato, Ptolemy, Pliny, Censorinus, Boethius, Augustine, Albert the Great, Pseudo-Dionysius, Hermes Trismegistus, Aratus, Virgil, Manilius, and Kabbalistic teaching on the sefirot (Keter and Hokhmah as the two unknowable "measures"), together with an extensive catena of scriptural proof-texts (Job, Proverbs, Isaiah, Ezekiel, Psalms, Wisdom, Jeremiah, John, Paul). The section argues that divine harmony is perceived not by bodily hearing but by the purified intellect, that all things return to God as to their final end, that the six-day creation encodes the structure of the perfect diapason (six tones, seven intervals, eighth as completion), and that the cycles of history through six ages lead humanity back to divine rest in the true sabbath, which is Christ.
essay:
# SECONDO L'ARMONIA DEL DIAPASON (pp. 1095–1206)

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of this very long section (21 chapters, approximately 42,000 words). The essay is based on a close reading of the first roughly 500 lines of the chunk, supplemented by the chapter-heading structure surveyed throughout.*

## Overview and Central Argument

The section opens by announcing that, having demonstrated the harmonic structure of the "worldly dwelling" (the cosmos) and its enneadic organisation, Zorzi now turns to making its *melodia* resound — the melody that derives from the "living and immutable breath of the Artificer." The cosmos is not merely structured harmonically; it is actively played as an instrument by God, whose breath (*spiritus*) fills and animates all things. The twenty-one chapters that follow elaborate this cosmological music across three registers: the harmony of the elements, the harmony of the spheres, and the harmony of the angelic intelligences — all of which converge in the diapason (the octave) as the symbol of divine completeness and return.

## Chapter I: God Completes All Things with His Breath

Zorzi opens by citing the Orphic line (preserved in Greek):

> «Tu accordi tutto il cielo sulla cetra sonora»

<cite>p. 1095 / line 11</cite>

This Orphic testimony is immediately reinforced by the Pythagorean Dorilao (the world is God's musical instrument), Alexander of Miletus, and Gregory Nazianzen (who praises the world's harmony of contraries). Timaeus of Locri, Apuleio, and Augustine's *De ordine* are adduced to affirm that the universe maintains an "indissoluble symmetry" in which contrary forces — male and female voices, treble and bass — resolve into one song under a divine choirmaster.

Zorzi then introduces the three levels of Boethian *musica*: *mundana* (elemental), *spherarum* (planetary), and angelic — the soul must purify its inner "eye" to perceive them. The key scriptural catena here is drawn from the Book of Job, read as a treatise on cosmic harmony:

> «Dov'eri tu quando io ponevo le fondamenta della terra? Chi ha fissato le sue dimensioni, chi ha teso su di essa la misura? [...] ciò fu fatto da me con tale armonia che le stelle del mattino esultano e gioiscono tutti i figli di Elohim»

<cite>Job 38:4–7, pp. 1096–1097</cite>

Proverbs 8 (the speech of Wisdom at creation) is cited in the Hebrew, with the technical gloss that the word *sha'ashua'* (שַׁעֲשׁוּעַ), rendered in the Vulgate as "delitias," means more precisely "delighting in the harmony of the universe." Ezekiel's vision of the *ophannim* (אוֹפַנִּים, the wheels) and the divine name *Shadday* (שַׁדַּי) — which Zorzi prefers to translate as "sufficient cause" or "God fully at work" rather than merely "Almighty" — are deployed to show the Spirit of life in the cosmic wheels. Albert the Great is cited for the doctrine that cosmic proportion was "devised by the Creator's wisdom, received from the source of supramundane wisdom."

## Chapter II: Who Is This God, and What Can We Know of Him?

This chapter turns epistemological. Zorzi argues that the two supreme Kabbalistic *sefirot* — Keter (called "source, crown, origin") and Hokhmah ("wisdom, firstborn") — are unknowable even to Moses, who could not pass through the "fiftieth gate of the preceding measure":

> «le due dimensioni supreme, ovvero le due dignità divine, la prima delle quali chiamano sorgente, corona, origine [...] non furono conosciute dai mortali, neanche dallo stesso Mosè, il quale (secondo quanto affermano) non poté attraversare la cinquantesima porta della dimensione precedente»

<cite>pp. 1097–1098 / lines 248–251</cite>

Only Christ, the Son, can reveal the Father (Matthew 11:27; John 1:18). Isaiah's triple repetition of "I" (*ego ... ego primus ... ego novissimus*) is explicated via Hebrew grammar to signal the Trinity hidden within the divine unity. The Peripatetic method of knowledge (definition, division, composition) is declared wholly inadequate for theology, which requires either direct prophetic *intuitio* (Isaiah, Ezekiel, Daniel, John, Paul, Stephen) or a middle way — the conformity (*convenientia*) of revealed truth with the purified intellect, illustrated by the metaphor of a key that fits its lock.

## Chapter III: All Things Truly Tend toward God

The circular motion of the spheres, the cycles of generation and corruption, and the Ecclesiastes verse about rivers returning to their source all demonstrate that things return to their origin. Zorzi distinguishes the return of the spiritual part of man (immediately to God on death) from the return of the earthy part (to earth, thence eventually to God). He cites Aristotle's doctrine of God as *desideratum*, but insists it is an insufficient account; the fuller picture requires the Neoplatonic and Kabbalistic understanding of emanation and return.

## Chapters IV–VI: The Appetite, Curriculum, and Drivers of Return

These chapters develop what draws created things back to God: natural appetite (chapters IV–V) and the angelic intelligences who act as horses drawing the chariot of the soul (chapter VI, *A quibus trahatur iste currus*). The *merkavah* (chariot) imagery of Ezekiel is explicitly invoked.

## Chapter VII: Scriptural Confirmation

A systematic dossier of scriptural proof-texts confirming the cosmological thesis, drawing especially on Paul, John, Jeremiah, Isaiah, Aggeus, and Wisdom.

## Chapter VIII: How All Things Go Forth from God through the Senarius

The number six, first perfect number and triangular, is the structure through which God "went forth" into creation. Zorzi identifies the six days of creation with the six tones of the diapason:

> «Quell'armonia diapasonica perfetta, quella cioè composta di sei elementi con sette intervalli, cui fa seguito l'ottavo che completa il diapason»

<cite>p. 975 / lines 1311–1313</cite>

The first three days of creation establish the elemental foundations (supracelestial, waters, earth); the second three days ornament creation with its inhabitants. The senarius squared gives 36; multiplied by the denarius it produces 360 — the degrees of the zodiac. The 365 days of the solar year correspond to the number of limbs in the human body.

## Chapters IX–XI: The Sabbath, the Six Ages, and Return

Chapter IX identifies the true *quies* (rest) and true sabbath as the goal of the return. Chapters X–XI map the six ages of human history (following Augustine and the Kabbalistic understanding of the six lower *sefirot*) as the senario through which humanity is led back into God, with the seventh day/age as sabbath.

## Chapter XII: Christ as True Rest and Sabbath

Christ (*Christus est vera requies, et sabbatum*) fulfils and consummates the cosmic return, bringing the sixth age to completion in the seventh rest.

## Chapters XIII–XVI: Completion of the Diapason in the Octonary; the Harmony of the Spheres

The *diapason* reaches its completion in the number eight (chapter XIV: *Consummatur perfectum diapason in octonario*). Chapters XV–XVI are explicitly musicological: the celestial spheres produce the diapason in their revolution (chapter XV) and Zorzi analyses the harmony of the spheres from both the perspective of the distances and the consonance of movements between the planets (chapter XVI, *De concentu coelorum tam ex distantia, quam ex consonantia motuum*), drawing on Pythagoras, Timaeus, Ptolemy, Pliny, and Censorinus.

## Chapters XVII–XXI: Planetary Harmony, the Zodiac, and Angelic Concord

Chapter XVII treats the "consonant fecundity" of the number seven in the planets; chapters XVIII–XIX concern the zodiacal signs and those who deny celestial influence. Chapter XX (*De concordia coelorum cum supernis mentibus*) argues for the concordance of the heavens with the angelic intelligences. The section closes (chapter XXI) with the thesis that all things lead back to God because they receive all their influence from the same source, even if the channels of influence appear diverse.

## Sources and Technical Vocabulary

Key ancient sources throughout: Orphic *Hymns*; Pythagoras; Timaeus of Locri; Plato (*Timaeus*); Ptolemy; Pliny (*Naturalis Historia*); Censorinus; Boethius (*De Musica*; *Consolatio*); Augustine (*De ordine*, *De Musica*, *De Genesi*); Albert the Great; Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (*De divinis nominibus*); Hermes Trismegistus (*Quod Deus latens simul ac patens sit*); Virgil (*Aeneid* VI, the *Spiritus intus alit* passage); Manilius (*Astronomica*); Aratus.

Kabbalistic technical terms introduced or used: *sefirot*; Keter (כֶּתֶר, "crown"); Hokhmah (חָכְמָה, "wisdom"); *ophannim* (אוֹפַנִּים, "wheels"); *sha'ashua'* (שַׁעֲשׁוּעַ, "delight/harmony"); Shadday (שַׁדַּי); the "fifty gates of understanding" (*shishim sha'are binah*). Musical-theoretical terms: *diapason*, *antiparistisis*, *diastemata*, *concentus*, *modulatio*, *senarius*, *octonarius*.

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

This section is the cosmological summit of the *De Harmonia Mundi*: the diapason that structures the title of the work is here fully explicated as both a musical-mathematical reality (six tones, seven intervals, octave completion) and a theological one (the six ages of history, the seventh rest, the eighth day of resurrection and return). The Kabbalistic reading of the sefirot as unknowable "measures" above Moses's reach, combined with the Christian identification of the Son as the sole revealer of the Father, exemplifies Zorzi's characteristic fusion of Renaissance Kabbalah with Christological Neoplatonism.
